← the Atlas

OpenCode v2 Perf — What Kolu Can Adapt

analysis · seedling ·proposed ·JUN 14, 2026

OpenCode Desktop v2's "10×" is a streaming-markdown number. Mapped onto Kolu's actual surfaces, most of it has no consumer — but the Pierre upgrade is a near-free win.

Contents

  • The 10× is workload-specific
  • Why most of it has no consumer in Kolu
  • What transfers
  • Confirmed facts & the open question

A read of OpenCode Desktop v2’s perf release (≈4 → 45.7 FPS under a 30× CPU throttle) against Kolu’s real architecture. Method: 13 agents — external research ▸ code-map ▸ synthesize ▸ adversarially verify; every load-bearing fact re-checked against source + the npm registry.

Verdict — the number is workload-specific

OpenCode’s win is a streaming-markdown message timeline number. Kolu has no such surface: agent output is the xterm.js WebGL terminal, the only transcript is a static SSR export, and code/diffs are already virtualized inside Pierre. So 4 of their 6 techniques have no consumer here. What transfers is narrow: bump @pierre/diffs 1.2.1 → 1.2.10 (≈2.3× faster parser, 9 releases of fixes, worker substrate — and Kolu’s Shiki 3.23.0 already satisfies the peer, so no forced Shiki 4 bump). Then optional worker-offload + a canvas rAF fix.

The 10× is workload-specific

OpenCode’s six techniques: (1) migrate the message timeline to TanStack Virtual with chat anchoring (anchorTo:'end', followOnAppend); (2) flicker / scroll-jump fixes (overflow-anchor:none + a manual rAF visual-anchor, and an absolute — not delta — scrollbar-thumb mapping); (3) bump Pierre 1.2.10 + Shiki 4.2.0; (4) Shiki → Web Worker (stream-tokenize the new suffix, reply with compact [content, style] tuples); (5) append-only assumptions (a text-delta accumulator bug that was the bogus 4-FPS baseline, plus freeze-all- prior-messages-only-the-last-mutates); (6) perf-trace CPU work — profile under 30× throttle, optimize p95/p99 frame time, and log rejected experiments (their token-batching and height-estimator ideas regressed the tail).

The headline number is a dev-profiling figure on a long, growing list of variable-height markdown/diff messages. That list is the thing being virtualized, anchored, and worker-highlighted — it’s the whole game.

Why most of it has no consumer in Kolu

Kolu’s perf-relevant surfaces, colored by verdict — amber = adopt, green = already covered / N-A, teal = the live agent stream:

  • #1, #2 (virtualize + scroll-anchor a message list) — no growing list to virtualize. Code/diffs are already windowed inside Pierre’s CodeView (inverse-sticky, binary-searched position→line, browser overflow-anchor already disabled, own scrollbar). The flicker/scroll fixes live inside Pierre — Kolu inherits them via R1.
  • #5 (append-only token accumulator) — no streaming text-delta accumulator. Agent state arrives as snapshot-then-full-replacement; Markdown.tsx takes a complete string and re-renders only when the whole string changes.
  • #4 markdown worker — highlight.ts is already the good version: a dynamic-imported singleton, highlighting each completed block once (never per-token). A markdown Shiki worker here would be over-engineering. The worker win is the diff path (R2), not markdown.

What transfers

AdaptationImpactEffort
R1Upgrade @pierre/diffs 1.2.1 → 1.2.10highmedium
R2Pierre worker pool + shiki-js engine for the diff pathhighmedium
R4rAF-coalesce the canvas wheel pan/zoom write-stormmediumlow
R3Active-gesture FPS / p95-p99 frame-time harnessmediummedium

High R1 — Bump @pierre/diffs to 1.2.10 (the standout)

Pinned ^1.2.1 in solid-pierre, client, transcript-html; 9 patch releases behind. Brings ≈2.3× faster parsePatchFiles for free to every diff surface (the 50k-line lockfile diffs of #809/#514) plus the worker substrate R2 needs. fileGap is used nowhere → the fileGap→spacing rename is a no-op; pierreTheme.ts already uses the --*-override convention → theming migration is aligned. Re-validate the PIERRE_DIFFS_LINE_HEIGHT=16 virtualizer contract (#1026) after the bump. Update the stale pierre/SKILL.md pin.

Medium R2 — Pierre worker pool + shiki-js, for the diff path only (depends on R1)

1.2.10 ships the worker substrate OpenCode built by hand: ./worker, ./worker/worker.js, ./worker/worker-portable.js (confirmed on npm). For an Electron client, preferredHighlighter:'shiki-js' also drops the Oniguruma WASM payload. Not a one-liner: CodeView.tsx’s buildOptions() passes no highlighter/worker option and CodeViewProps doesn’t expose one — the wrapper needs a prop added and threaded, and the exact 1.2.10 CodeViewOptions field must be read from the installed type defs. shiki-js is not universally faster — validate against Kolu’s curated languages first.

Medium R4 — rAF-coalesce the canvas wheel write-storm (quick win)

This is OpenCode’s real principle — per-frame work proportional to new data — applied to Kolu’s actual hot loop, the canvas. gestures.ts writes panX/panY/zoom synchronously per raw wheel event → a 120-event fling over 20 tiles = ~2,400 transform writes (#1308’s measured 8–24×). Accumulate the delta, flush once per requestAnimationFrame. #1308 found it latent/benign and deferred it pending a repro — so gate it behind R3, reproduce under throttle first. Mind the 150 ms wheel-ownership timeout.

Medium R3 — Active-gesture FPS / p99 harness (methodology)

The CDP tracer (docs/perf-investigations/scripts/tile-aura/trace.js) is idle-window, aggregate-only; no FPS harness, no performance.mark/measure in hot paths (it already enables blink.user_timing, just unused). Drive an active pan/zoom under setCPUThrottlingRate, capture p95/p99 + dropped frames, and log rejected experiments. dock-1308/kolu-driver.js already proves active-gesture tracing works. This is what makes R4 measured, not guessed.

Sequencing: R1 → R3 (so R2/R4 are measured) → R2 and R4 behind that evidence.

Confirmed facts & the open question

The verification pass settled every load-bearing claim:

  • @pierre/diffs pinned ^1.2.1 in 3 packages; installed 1.2.1; @pierre/trees 1.0.0-beta.4; shiki 3.23.0. verified
  • npm latest = 1.2.10; peer shiki ^3 || ^4 → satisfied by 3.23.0, so the Pierre bump does not force Shiki 4. 1.2.10 ships the three ./worker exports. verified
  • fileGap used nowhere (rename = no-op); pierreTheme.ts already on the --*-override convention. verified
  • @pierre/trees has no newer release → leave on beta.4. verified

The one known-unknown — by design, not a gap

The exact CodeViewOptions field that threads Pierre’s worker pool / preferredHighlighter through Kolu’s CodeView wrapper can only be read from the installed 1.2.10 type defs — this worktree has no node_modules. The worker entry points are confirmed on npm; the precise wiring is an implementation-time confirmation (R2), recorded here rather than overstated.
